artifacts.content:
Laura,

I’m still at the office staring at this damn snowplow agenda packet and thought I’d just write it all out to you instead of pacing the halls. On paper it’s just “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet,” but in my head it’s everything that’s happened the last few years piled into one truck.

I keep thinking about that December 23–27, 2022 blizzard on the east side by Brainerd High School, when we were short a plow because I didn’t get Unit #204 repaired in time. Karen Schultz dropped that written reprimand in my personnel file on January 10, 2023, and I still feel it every time a storm shows up on the radar. Then we rolled right into Sophie’s emergency appendectomy at Cuyuna on October 19, 2023, and the $4,300 we’re still paying off, and Evan’s ACL surgery at Essentia in February 2024 with the $18,700 bill. It’s like every time work blows up, something hits us at home too.

And now I’m trying to keep this new truck at $278,465.29 so we don’t push the levy and our own escrow at 1427 Pine Ridge Lane up again. I know the Council only sees “Capital Equipment Fund – $280,000 programmed,” not the mortgage or Sophie’s meds or Evan’s hockey fees.

I’m also still annoyed that my step increase got held up last March because snow overtime was $22,000 over, mostly thanks to 204 dying every other storm. Karen’s April 3, 2024 “budget management” talk is burned into my brain.

Anyway, I’ll be home after I finish double‑checking the numbers one more time. Don’t wait up if you’re tired.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Why this snowplow thing feels so heavy

artifacts.content:
Dan,

Long day, so I’m dumping my brain here. I just finished tweaking the staff report on the 2026 tandem‑axle plow for NorthStar Municipal Equipment. On the surface it’s simple: $278,465.29 from the 2026 Capital Equipment Fund, declare Unit #204 surplus, move on. But you know how much baggage is tied up in that truck.

I still replay that week of January 8, 2025 on East River Road, when 204 died mid‑event and we ended up with that $3,600 claim after Mark and Janice’s neighbor slid their Buick into the utility pole. Same winter, the February 5, 2024 storm where the hydraulic line blew by Brainerd Medical Center and Sophie sat at St. Joe’s Elementary almost 90 minutes past dismissal because I was babysitting a dead truck. Try explaining that to an 11‑year‑old.

Then there’s the personal money stuff I never talk about at work. We’re still digging out from Sophie’s October 19, 2023 appendectomy at Cuyuna – about $4,300 left on the payment plan – plus Evan’s ACL surgery in February 2024 at Essentia, that $18,700 bill. Every time someone suggests deferring the plow and doing a mid‑season budget amendment for more repairs, all I can picture is another levy bump and our escrow at Pine Ridge jumping again.

I know I’m oversensitive after Jennifer Lang from CliftonLarsonAllen brought up my old $9,800 credit card judgment during the October 2025 audit fieldwork at City Hall. She basically hinted it could “look bad” if I pushed big‑ticket equipment. So now I’m second‑guessing every word I write about this truck even though it’s clearly the right call.

Anyway, thanks for letting me rant. How’s your week at Fleet Farm?

– Mike

metadata.subject: All the junk tied to this one plow truck

artifacts.content:
Personal notes – Unit #204 replacement context (do NOT share all this at work)

• Downtime/repairs: Over the last three winters (2022–2025), 204 has been down 41 days. February 2023 alone we rented a plow from Lakes Area Equipment for 9 days at $1,950 and paid Brainerd Truck & Trailer $8,400 for engine work. I maxed my purchasing card to cover the rental and deposit and we ended up postponing the March 2023 Duluth trip to see Mark and Janice.

• Family emergencies tied to breakdowns:
  – Feb 5, 2024: hydraulic failure near Brainerd Medical Center; Sophie left waiting at St. Joseph’s Elementary ~90 minutes.
  – Dec 27, 2024: stall on County Road 3; Evergreen Senior Living (Mom & Joan) unplowed nearly 7 hours, aides couldn’t get in.
  – Jan 3, 2025: starter died at Washington & 8th right as Evan texted about Mom’s fall at Evergreen; stuck nearly 2 hours waiting for Brainerd Truck & Trailer while Dan left Mills Fleet Farm to get her to Essentia’s ER.

• Health/workload: After the mild heart attack on November 18, 2023, Dr. Emily Larson at Brainerd Heart & Vascular told me no more 12–14‑hour plow shifts. Hard to square that with the March 1–3, 2024 storm when Jason Miller was suspended after his Feb 26 failed drug test and I ended up doing a 28‑hour stretch on Highway 210 babysitting 204.

• Career fallout: December 23–27, 2022 blizzard east side failure → written reprimand from Karen Schultz on Jan 10, 2023. March 2024 OT overage (~$22k) tied to 204 downtime → April 3, 2024 “budget management” review and delayed step increase.

Remember: all of this is WHY the 2026 replacement is non‑negotiable, even if I only talk publicly about lifecycle costs and reliability.

metadata.title: Unit #204 – private background log
metadata.location: Public Works office – Michael’s desk drawer

artifacts.content:
Ellen,

I wanted to connect a couple of threads before we get too deep into spring scheduling.

First, on the family side: my intermittent FMLA for Sophie’s occupational therapy in Duluth technically wrapped up last summer, but her needs have shifted. After her February 2025 ADHD and sensory processing eval with Dr. Megan Larson at St. Luke’s Pediatric Rehab, we did the 6–8 weeks of weekly OT starting April 7, 2025. Now, on top of that, she’s been in weekly CBT with Dr. Megan Schultz at Essentia–Brainerd since November 2025 for pretty severe storm‑related anxiety and panic attacks. On the January 9 and 18 snowfalls I stayed home until around 2:00–3:00 a.m. getting her through meds and breathing exercises before heading into the Public Works garage, which is why I was late rolling in.

Layer that onto my own health stuff – the mild heart attack on November 18, 2023, and Dr. Emily Larson at Brainerd Heart & Vascular telling me to avoid 12–14‑hour plow shifts – and I’m worried about trying to keep the same on‑call load if we have another winter like 2022–2023.

Operationally, that all intersects with Unit #204 limping along. When it died twice on the College Drive route during the February 2025 blizzard, I logged 26 hours back‑to‑back and barely saw 1427 Pine Ridge Lane for three days. We even had to pay Mom’s in‑home aide from Good Neighbor an extra $540 for overnights because Laura couldn’t leave Sophie and Evan alone to check on her.

I’m not asking for special treatment, just some realistic adjustments to on‑call expectations while we get through this season and (hopefully) get the 2026 replacement truck in service.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: On‑call expectations, health, and family logistics

artifacts.content:
Event: Council P&F Committee + Main Agenda – Snowplow Replacement Presentation

Date: 2026-02-09
Time: 6:00–7:30 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees (for my planning): Council, City Administrator Karen Olson, City Attorney Mark Jensen, PW staff as needed

Notes for myself (not for packet):

• Title on agenda: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet.” Keep it clean and boring, even though in my head it’s tied to a lot more – Dec 23–27, 2022 blizzard reprimand, March 2024 overtime overrun (~$22k), the 41 days of downtime for Unit #204 since 2022, and that awful Jan 7–9, 2025 stretch when Northern Pines sat unplowed for nearly 36 hours and Linda McCarthy threatened a negligence claim.

• Be ready if anyone asks about why we’re pushing to stay under the $280,000 line item. Quietly, that’s about levy pressure and people like us at 1427 Pine Ridge Lane already juggling Evan’s $18,700 ACL bill from Feb 2024 and Sophie’s $4,300 left from her Oct 19, 2023 appendectomy at Cuyuna.

• Remember Jennifer Lang’s October 2025 audit comment about my 2023 $9,800 credit card judgment and how it could “look” if I’m the face of big capital purchases. Stay calm, stick to lifecycle costs, reliability, and safety.

• Aim to keep the actual presentation to 15 minutes, then Q&A. Bring downtime chart, repair cost summary (including Feb 2023 $1,950 rental + $8,400 engine repair), and map of school/senior routes most affected when 204 dies.

Wear clean shirt, bring extra coffee.

Laura,

I know we already talked about money over dinner, but I wanted to put this down in writing so we can look at it together this weekend.

Work side first: the new tandem‑axle plow quote from NorthStar came in at $278,465.29, just under the $280,000 we programmed in the 2026 Capital Equipment Fund. On paper that looks great. In reality I’m haunted by what happens if Council decides to kick it to 2027 and pour more cash into Unit #204 instead. We’ve already had the March 2024 overtime blow‑out – about $22,000 over – that froze my step increase until July after the April 3 “budget management” grilling. If 204 keeps failing, we’ll be right back in mid‑season budget amendment territory.

And when that happens, the levy creeps up. Which means our escrow at 1427 Pine Ridge Lane creeps up. After Sophie’s October 19, 2023 appendectomy at Cuyuna (still ~$4,300 on the plan) and Evan’s February 2024 ACL reconstruction at Essentia ($18,700), plus the $540 we paid Good Neighbor for Mom’s extra overnights during the February 2025 blizzard when I was on 26 straight hours because 204 died twice on College Drive… there’s just not much slack left.

I keep thinking about that week of January 8, 2025 on East River Road too, when 204 went down and we ate that $3,600 property damage claim after the neighbor’s Buick slid into the pole. It’s all the same story: keep limping the truck along, pay more in the long run.

Maybe Sunday afternoon we can sit down with the budget, see where we are on Sophie’s $4,300, and what a potential escrow jump would do. I don’t want to drag you into work stress, but it all blurs together.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Plow purchase, levy talk, and our budget

artifacts.content:
Me: Hey, just got home. Kids in bed?

Laura: Just now. Sophie finally settled around 2:15. Storm nights are rough.

Me: Yeah. I was thinking about how different this winter feels compared to that December 23–27, 2022 blizzard when we blew it on the east side by the high school and Karen slapped that written reprimand in my file on Jan 10, 2023.

Laura: You’ve carried that one a long time.

Me: Hard not to, especially after Nov 18, 2023 when my heart decided to join the party and Dr. Emily Larson said “no more 12–14 hour plow shifts.” Then of course I did a 28‑hour run on Hwy 210 during the March 1–3, 2024 storm because Jason Miller was suspended after that Feb 26 failed drug test and 204 kept dying.

Laura: You scared us with that one.

Me: I scared myself. And while all that was happening, we had Sophie’s appendectomy at Cuyuna on Oct 19, 2023 (still owe about $4,300), Evan’s ACL in Feb 2024 at Essentia ($18,700), and Mom’s extra aide hours during the Feb 2025 blizzard when I logged 26 hours straight because 204 broke down twice on College Drive. Remember the extra $540 to Good Neighbor that week?

Laura: Hard to forget.

Me: All of that is why I’m pushing so hard to get this 2026 replacement plow approved at $278,465.29. I’m tired of gambling our health and our bank account on a 2007 truck.

Laura: I get it. Let’s just make sure you’re around for the kids, okay?

Me: Trying. One storm at a time.
